title: Can and Should You Get Life Insurance on a Child?
excerpt: Few financial topics generate more mixed feelings than life insurance on a child. It's not widely understood,
content:
Few financial topics generate more mixed feelings than life insurance on a child. It's not widely understood, and the conversation is often colored by discomfort with the subject or enthusiastic marketing that can overcomplicate the decision.

Yes, you can get life insurance on a child. The most common form is a whole life policy, which provides a small death benefit (the original purpose) but also builds cash value over time. Premiums are very low because the actuarial risk is low: Children rarely die. These policies can typically be converted to adult coverage later without a medical exam. It’s one of the more commonly cited reasons parents buy them: To lock in insurability for a child who might develop a health condition later.

What it's not: Child life insurance is not a college savings plan, not an investment vehicle, and not a substitute for a 529 account or Roth IRA for a minor. The cash value grows slowly and is often less efficient than other savings tools when evaluated on a pure returns basis. It's also not a financial priority for most families: Insuring the income earners in a household almost always should take precedence.

That said, there are scenarios where child life insurance makes sense: a family history of medical conditions that could affect future insurability, a desire to guarantee lifetime coverage at a very low rate, or as a financial legacy vehicle in specific estate planning contexts.

The decision is less about whether child life insurance is “good or bad” and more about whether it fits your specific priorities and whether the premium dollars could be doing more work elsewhere.

Child life insurance is legitimate and, in the right context, genuinely useful. But it's frequently oversold as a savings or investment product when it's primarily an insurance product. The insurability argument has real merit for some families—but it's worth asking whether you've first maximized coverage on the adults whose income the household depends on. For most families, that step should come first.

title: Your Health Insurance Options When You're Living or Traveling Abroad
excerpt: More people are living, working, and spending extended time outside the U.S. than at any point in recent
content:
More people are living, working, and spending extended time outside the U.S. than at any point in recent history. But health insurance coverage is one of the most misunderstood parts of that transition and the gaps can be expensive.

First, the baseline: Most U.S.-based health insurance plans, including those through employers, the ACA marketplace, and Medicare, provide little to no coverage for care received abroad. Medicare, for example, generally doesn't cover services outside the United States at all. An ER visit or hospitalization in another country is likely to be paid entirely out of pocket if you're relying on a standard domestic plan.

For people spending significant time overseas, there are a few distinct types of coverage to understand. Travel insurance (short-term) typically covers emergency medical care and evacuation during trips up to a few weeks or months, but it's not designed for routine care. International health insurance (longer-term expatriate plans) functions more like traditional coverage, including preventive care, specialist visits, and sometimes dental, and is designed for people living abroad for extended periods. Some countries also allow foreigners to participate in national health systems, either for free or at low cost, depending on visa status and length of stay.

The decision framework here depends on how long you'll be away, whether you have a home base in the U.S. you intend to return to, and whether you want coverage for emergencies only or more comprehensive care. There's no one-size-fits-all answer, but ignoring the coverage gap entirely is a risk that tends to be underestimated until something goes wrong.

Coverage abroad is genuinely more complex than most people expect, and the stakes are high: A serious medical event in another country can cost tens of thousands of dollars out of pocket. The good news is that international health insurance has become more accessible and more competitive. The tricky part is that the right solution varies significantly based on your destination, duration, and home-country obligations. It's worth doing the research before you go, not after you’ve arrived.

title: What IRS Tax Relief Services Actually Do and What to Watch Out For
excerpt: If you owe back taxes, you've probably seen advertisements for tax relief companies promising to “settle
content:
If you owe back taxes, you've probably seen advertisements for tax relief companies promising to “settle your debt for pennies on the dollar.” The industry is real, but the difference between what's advertised and what's actually available is wide and worth understanding before you make any decisions.

The IRS does offer several legitimate programs for taxpayers who can't pay in full. These include installment agreements (paying over time), currently not collectible status (a pause on collections when you have no ability to pay), and an Offer in Compromise, which is the formal process by which the IRS agrees to settle a tax debt for less than the full amount owed. Penalty abatement is also available in certain cases, particularly for first-time issues or reasonable cause.

Here's what the marketing often leaves out: Offers in Compromise are not easy to get approved. The IRS accepted roughly 30–40% of applications in recent years, and those that qualified typically involved people with very limited income, minimal assets, and no realistic path to paying the full amount. Most people who owe back taxes don't qualify and may be better served by an installment agreement instead.

Tax relief companies can legitimately help navigate this process, especially if your situation is complex. But you can also access the same programs directly through the IRS website, often for free or through free tax assistance programs. The question isn't whether relief exists but whether the services charging hundreds or thousands of dollars are providing value proportional to their fees.

If you're facing a tax debt, the first step is almost always understanding what you actually owe and what tax debt programs you may qualify for before engaging any third party.

Tax relief is real, and so is the industry built around it, which ranges from genuinely helpful to outright predatory. The IRS programs themselves are worth understanding directly, because many people hire help for processes they could navigate on their own. At the same time, for complex multi-year situations or small business tax issues, professional help often pays for itself. The key is knowing enough to evaluate what you're being offered before signing anything.

title: A Financial Checklist to Help You Feel in Control
content: During times of uncertainty, being proactive about your finances is one of the best ways to regain a sense of control. Here are the to-do's you may have been putting off, most of which can be done online without leaving your home.

items.label: Write out your financial goals to stay focused and motivated
Set priorities if you're not sure where to start
Start planning toward a specific goal like paying down debt, building savings, or setting up automatic deposits from your paycheck
Update or create a spending plan
Use an app to track spending and compare it to your plan
Submit any pending HSA or FSA reimbursement receipts
Explore employee benefit discount programs you may not be using
Review your cell phone plan to make sure you're getting the best deal
Research competitive quotes for auto and home/renter's insurance
Explore community groups and local Buy-Nothing Groups as alternatives to buying certain items
Enroll at CreditKarma.com or request your free credit report at AnnualCreditReport.com
Dispute any errors directly through CreditKarma or with the credit reporting agencies
Consider opening and contributing to a traditional or Roth IRA
Open a spousal IRA for a nonworking spouse, if applicable
Decide what to do with old retirement accounts from former employers
Enroll at SSA.gov and review your Social Security benefit estimates
File your taxes
Review tax-planning strategies now to improve next year's outcome
Start or revisit your estate plan using an online tool or estate planning attorney
Make sure potential caregivers know where important documents are located
Review and update beneficiaries on life insurance policies and retirement accounts
Build your investing confidence: start with a beginner's guide to investing
Use this as an opportunity to share money lessons with your kids

title: Having a Baby: Health Insurance Checklist
content: Even with good insurance, pregnancy brings new out-of-pocket costs. Use this checklist to find out what's covered, and what's not, so you can plan ahead and focus on what really matters: your growing family.

items.label: I’ve reviewed what my insurance will cover for my pregnancy and delivery
I know my annual maximum out-of-pocket cost
I’ve checked into healthy-pregnancy programs or birthing classes offered by my provider or insurer

sections.title: If You Need More Than the Basics

items.label: I’ve determined whether I need preauthorization for any prenatal tests or procedures
I know how to get preauthorization if I need it
I know how to determine what I'll pay for a specific procedure
I have looked into coverage for specialized care, or if I have complications during pregnancy or birth
I’ve determined whether I need preauthorization for my delivery or hospital stay
I know how many days are covered for recovery, including a cesarean birth
I’ve considered what happens if I'm billed for a non-network provider I didn't choose (like a lab or anesthesiologist)
I’ve considered my options for where and how I deliver (e.g., a hospital, birthing center, or at home)
I know what I need to do to make sure my baby is covered under my plan following birth
I am aware of deadlines for adding my baby to my policy
I have reviewed coverage for my baby if they need to stay longer in the hospital
I know how my plan covers lactation support or breastfeeding supplies

tip.title: Now breathe
tip.description: A quick call to your insurance provider can save you hours of stress and thousands of dollars. Get clarity on what's covered, keep good records, and focus on what's truly priceless: bringing your baby home safe and sound.

title: Do You Need a Living Trust?
content: A revocable living trust can make settling your estate simpler, faster, and more private, but it isn't right for everyone. Answer these 7 quick questions and find out if it might be worth considering.
excerpt: A revocable living trust can make settling your estate simpler, faster, and more private, but it isn't right for everyone. Answer these 7 quick questions and find out if it might be worth considering.

questions.questionText: Do you or your spouse have children from a previous relationship?
questions.tip: It lets you decide who inherits what and when they get it. In blended families, the stakes around inheritance can get complicated fast.
questions.explanation: A trust gives you the flexibility to specify exactly who receives what and when. For example, your spouse could receive income from the trust while your children from a previous relationship receive the balance later. This level of control can be difficult to achieve with a will alone.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you want your estate to stay private and avoid court delays?
questions.tip: Wills go through probate, a public and sometimes lengthy court process that can take months or even years to resolve.
questions.explanation: Assets titled in a living trust bypass probate entirely and transfer privately to your beneficiaries. One important detail: a trust only controls what you place in it, so remember to re-title accounts and real estate in the trust's name or the probate protection disappears.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you own significant assets outside of retirement accounts that are not jointly owned with your spouse?
questions.tip: Assets like savings accounts, investment accounts, real estate, and business interests do not automatically transfer to your beneficiaries the way retirement accounts and jointly owned property do.
questions.explanation: If you have meaningful assets outside of retirement accounts or joint ownership, those assets will likely go through probate without a trust. A living trust can ensure they transfer quickly, privately, and exactly the way you intend.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you expect your estate to exceed $15 million ($30 million if married)?
questions.tip: Estates above the federal exemption threshold may be subject to estate taxes, and planning ahead can make a significant difference.
questions.explanation: While a living trust does not eliminate estate taxes on its own, it can be a useful tool for making the most of current exclusions and planning for larger estates. If your estate approaches or exceeds these thresholds, an estate planning attorney can walk you through tax-efficient strategies.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you own real estate or other property in more than one state?
questions.tip: Without a trust, your heirs could face separate probate proceedings in each state where you own property.
questions.explanation: Holding out-of-state property in a revocable trust streamlines the process significantly. Instead of multiple probate cases in multiple states, your assets transfer through a single, unified document.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you have a child or dependent with special needs?
questions.tip: Leaving assets directly to a person receiving government benefits like Medicaid or Supplemental Security Income can unintentionally disqualify them from those programs.
questions.explanation: A special needs trust, which can be structured within a revocable living trust, allows you to leave assets for a dependent's care without affecting their eligibility for government assistance. If this applies to your situation, it is one of the most important planning steps you can take.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

questions.questionText: Do you want to control when and how your heirs receive their inheritance?
questions.tip: A straightforward will transfers assets to beneficiaries immediately and without conditions. That works fine in many situations, but not all.
questions.explanation: A trust lets you set the terms. You can specify that a child receives a portion of their inheritance at 25, another portion at 30, or only for specific purposes like education or buying a home. If you have younger beneficiaries or want to build guardrails into your estate plan, a trust gives you tools a will simply cannot.

answers.answerText: Yes
No

criteria.label: A Living Trust May Be Worth Exploring
criteria.resultText: Based on your answers, your situation has one or more characteristics where a living trust could offer real advantages, whether that's protecting privacy, simplifying a complex family situation, managing out-of-state property, or controlling how your heirs receive their inheritance. That doesn't mean a trust is definitely the right move, but it is worth a closer look.
criteria.nextMove: Start by checking your workplace benefits. Your Employee Assistance Program (EAP) or legal benefits plan may include free or discounted access to estate planning tools and attorney consultations. From there, connecting with a financial coach is a great way to clarify your goals before you sit down with an attorney.

criteria.label: A Living Trust May Not Be Necessary Right Now
criteria.resultText: Based on your answers, your estate may be straightforward enough that a living trust adds more complexity and cost than it's worth. If most of your assets already pass directly to beneficiaries through retirement accounts, life insurance, or joint ownership, you may be well covered without one.
criteria.nextMove: Your next move: That said, every estate plan is different, and what works today may not work as your life evolves. A financial coach can help you take stock of what you have, identify any gaps in your current plan, and flag if and when a trust might make sense down the road.

title: Are You Ready to Be a Landlord?
content: The right rental property can grow your wealth, but the wrong one can waste your time and drain your cash. Answer 10 quick questions and find out exactly where you stand.
excerpt: The right rental property can grow your wealth, but the wrong one can waste your time and drain your cash. Answer 10 quick questions and find out exactly where you stand.

questions.questionText: Where does your debt situation stand right now?
questions.tip: High-interest debt like credit cards can eat into rental income fast and signal that your cash flow isn't ready for the added pressure of a property.
questions.explanation: Having no high-interest rate loans put you in the best position to be a landlord.

answers.answerText: I have credit card or other high-interest debt I'm still working through.
I'm mostly debt-free but still carrying a small balance or two.
I have no high-interest debt. My only debts are low-interest loans like a mortgage or car payment.

questions.questionText: What does your credit score look like?
questions.tip: Lenders typically require a 740+ credit score to qualify for the best investment property rates. A lower score doesn't disqualify you, but it will cost you more every month.
questions.explanation: Credit scores range from 300 to 850, with 740+ being the gold standard. Scores above 740 unlock the best interest rates on loans, credit cards, and mortgages, saving you thousands over your lifetime.

answers.answerText: I'm not sure, or it's below 680.
It's somewhere between 680 and 739.
It's 740 or higher.

questions.questionText: How prepared are you financially to actually buy and maintain a rental property?
questions.tip: Investment properties typically require a 20% down payment, and that's just the start. Repairs, vacancies, and unexpected costs are part of the deal.
questions.explanation: Ensure your cash reserve is large enough to cover the 20% down payment, estimations of repairs and renovations and other maintenance costs.

answers.answerText: I don't have enough saved for a down payment or much cushion beyond that.
I have a down payment plus some reserves, but a long vacancy or big repair would stretch me thin.
I have a 20% down payment, cash for repairs and renovations, and enough reserves to cover at least a year of vacancies and maintenance.

questions.questionText: How would you describe your retirement savings and workplace benefits situation?
questions.tip: Rental property is a great wealth builder, but not if it comes at the expense of tax-advantaged savings you can't get back. Maxing these out first is almost always the smarter move.
questions.explanation: Your retirement savings matters. Look to maximize your 401(k), IRA and applicable workplace benefits like HSA or FSA accounts.

answers.answerText: I haven't maxed out my retirement contributions or taken full advantage of my workplace benefits yet.
I'm contributing to retirement but not maxing it out, and I'm using some but not all of my available benefits.
I'm maxing out my 401(k), IRA, and any applicable workplace benefits like an HSA or FSA.

questions.questionText: How well do you know the rental market you're considering investing in?
questions.tip: The investors who get burned are usually the ones who skipped their homework. Knowing your market, average rents, vacancy rates, and neighborhood trends, is what separates a smart investment from an expensive guess.
questions.explanation: Understanding the current market for rental rates and expenses will put you in a better position for success.

answers.answerText: I haven't done much research yet.
I have a general sense of the market but haven't dug into the specific numbers yet.
I've researched average rents, vacancy rates, and neighborhood data and I know the numbers cold.

questions.questionText: Have you run the numbers on a specific property or properties?
questions.tip: A rental property is a business. The math needs to work before you fall in love with the deal.
questions.explanation: A detailed 12-month projection showing worst-case scenarios will help you maximize your chances of success as a landlord.

answers.answerText: Not yet. I'm still in the early stages of exploring.
I've done some back-of-the-napkin math but haven't built out a full income and expense projection.
I've run detailed 12-month projections including worst-case scenarios, and I've confirmed the gross monthly rent is at least 1% of the property value or I'm confident in my target cap rate.

questions.questionText: How would you handle the day-to-day responsibilities of owning a rental property?
questions.tip: Being a landlord is a second job. Whether you manage it yourself or hire help, you need a plan before a tenant calls at midnight with a broken furnace.
questions.explanation: Understanding your time and ability to manage the rental property yourself will be necessary even if your plan is to work with a property manager.

answers.answerText: I haven't thought much about this yet.
I'd plan to manage it myself but I'm not sure I have the time or I'm not confident in my ability to handle repairs and tenant issues.
I have a clear plan. Either I have the time and ability to manage it myself, or I've confirmed the numbers still work with a property manager factored in.

questions.questionText: How familiar are you with the legal and ownership considerations of being a landlord?
questions.tip: From LLC structures to landlord-tenant laws, the legal side of rental ownership can make or break your investment if you go in blind.
questions.explanation: Many States have landlord rules and rental housing compliance which must be maintained.

answers.answerText: I'm not familiar with any of this yet.
I have a basic awareness but haven't researched what applies to my specific state or situation.
I've researched ownership structures like LLCs, I understand the landlord-tenant laws in my state, and I know what I need to have in place before I sign anything.

questions.questionText: How would you describe your experience with real estate and your comfort with the risks involved?
questions.tip: Rental property is not a diversified investment. Owning one or two properties in one area is a concentrated bet, and going in with eyes open makes all the difference.
questions.explanation: Learn about landlord expectations and standards as much as you can.

answers.answerText: I'm new to real estate and still learning what I'm getting into.
I've owned a home for a few years or done some research into landlording, and I understand the basics of the risks involved.
I have meaningful real estate experience, I've read up on landlording, and I fully understand that a small number of properties in one area carries real concentration risk.

questions.questionText: Where do you stand with lender conversations and financing approval?
questions.tip: Knowing you can actually get the loan before you fall in love with a property saves a lot of heartache. Investment property financing has stricter requirements than a primary home purchase.
questions.explanation: Speaking with a lender before seeking approval is ideal to set you up well in advance of the application approval process.

answers.answerText: I haven't talked to a lender yet.
I have a general sense of what I'd qualify for but haven't had a formal conversation with a lender yet.
I've spoken with a lender, I understand the requirements for investment property financing, and I'm confident about my approval.

criteria.label: Not Quite Yet
criteria.resultText: You're not ready to be a landlord yet, and that's actually good news. Knowing where the gaps are now means you won't make an expensive mistake later. Rental property can be a powerful wealth builder, but only when the foundation is solid. Focus on wiping out high-interest debt, building your cash reserves, and locking in your retirement savings first. The opportunity will still be there when you're truly ready.
criteria.nextMove: Connect with a financial coach to build a clear roadmap to rental readiness.

criteria.label: Almost There
criteria.resultText: You've got a solid foundation and you're closer than you think. A few key gaps are standing between you and a smart first rental investment. The good news is they're closeable. Tighten up the areas where you're not quite at a 5 yet and you'll be in a genuinely strong position to make a move with confidence.
criteria.nextMove: Review your results and identify your weakest areas. A financial coach can help you close those gaps faster than you'd expect.

criteria.label: Landlord Ready
criteria.resultText: You've done the work. Your finances are in order, you know your market, and you understand what you're getting into. You're in a strong position to move forward on a rental property with real confidence. The next step isn't more research, it's action.
criteria.nextMove: Compare properties, pressure test your projections one more time, and connect with a financial coach to make sure your plan is airtight before you sign anything.

title: Tax Myths That Could Cost You
content: Einstein once said that the hardest thing in the world to understand is the income tax. He was not wrong. Taxes are one of the areas with the most misconceptions, and believing the wrong things can cost you real money. Take this 10-question quiz and find out how much you really know.
excerpt: Einstein once said that the hardest thing in the world to understand is the income tax. He was not wrong. Taxes are one of the areas with the most misconceptions, and believing the wrong things can cost you real money. Take this 10-question quiz and find out how much you really know.

questions.questionText: If your goal is to build wealth, your primary financial objective when it comes to taxes should be:
questions.explanation: Minimizing taxes sounds smart until you realize the logical endpoint is earning no income at all. The real goal is maximizing what you keep after taxes, not minimizing what you pay. The mortgage interest deduction is a classic example of this myth in action. While the deduction reduces the cost of interest, it does not eliminate it. Keeping a mortgage just to preserve a deduction means you are still paying interest to save a fraction of it in taxes. Do not let the tax tail wag the dog.

answers.answerText: Maximizing after-tax returns, even if that means paying more in taxes.
Avoiding any investment that creates a taxable event.
Minimizing your tax bill above all else.
Never paying off deductible debt like a mortgage so you keep the tax deduction.

questions.questionText: If you are in the 24% federal tax bracket, what percentage of your total income do you actually pay in federal taxes?
questions.explanation: The 24% bracket is your marginal rate, meaning you only pay 24% on the dollars that fall within that bracket. Everything below that threshold is taxed at lower rates. This is why most people pay a lower average tax rate than their marginal rate suggests. It also means that contributing to a 401(k) is especially valuable because those contributions come off the top of your income, avoiding your highest marginal rate, while withdrawals in retirement are often taxed at lower rates.

answers.answerText: Exactly 24% on all of your income.
Less than 24%, because only the income above the bracket threshold is taxed at that rate.
More than 24%, because higher earners pay a surcharge on their total income.
24% on your gross income before any deductions.

questions.questionText: What is the most effective way for the average person to reduce their tax bill?
questions.explanation: Most of what you can do to reduce your taxes happens before tax season, not during it. Contributing to tax-advantaged accounts like 401(k)s, IRAs, and HSAs is the single most impactful strategy available to most people. Tax software handles the filing for the average person just fine. Where a tax professional or financial advisor genuinely adds value is in planning throughout the year, especially if you own a business, investment property, or have complex non-sheltered investments.

answers.answerText: Hire a tax accountant to find loopholes before filing.
Delay filing until October to give yourself more time to find deductions.
Contribute to tax-advantaged accounts like 401(k)s, IRAs, and HSAs throughout the year.
Itemize every possible expense and submit receipts with your return.

questions.questionText: Who is usually responsible for paying the gift tax when a taxable gift is made?
questions.explanation: The gift tax is usually the giver's responsibility, not the recipient's. The reason the tax exists is to prevent people from using lifetime gifts to avoid paying estate tax. In most cases the recipient owes nothing, though there are a few exceptions. Gifts from an employer or in appreciation of your work may be taxable as income, and if you receive appreciated property you inherit the giver's original cost basis, meaning you could owe capital gains tax on the growth when you eventually sell it.

answers.answerText: The person who receives the gift.
Both the giver and the receiver split the tax equally.
Neither. Gifts are never taxable.
The person who gives the gift.

questions.questionText: In 2026, how much can you give to any one person in a single year without triggering a gift tax return?
questions.explanation: The annual gift tax exclusion is $19,000 per person in 2026. This means you can give $19,000 to as many individuals as you like without filing a gift tax return. Married couples can combine their exclusions to give $38,000 to a single recipient tax-free. Gifts to qualified charities, your spouse, or directly to a medical or educational institution on someone's behalf are unlimited and do not count toward this threshold.

questions.questionText: You want to help pay for a grandchild's college tuition without triggering gift tax implications. What is the most effective way to do it?
questions.explanation: Payments made directly to a qualified educational institution on someone's behalf are excluded from gift tax entirely and do not count against your annual exclusion or lifetime exemption. The same rule applies to direct payments to medical institutions for someone's healthcare. Writing the check to the school rather than to your grandchild is a simple but powerful strategy that many families overlook.

answers.answerText: Give your grandchild cash and let them pay the school directly.
Write the check directly to the educational institution rather than to your grandchild.
Split the gift with your spouse to stay under the annual exclusion.
Set up a payment plan with your grandchild to keep it under the annual limit.

questions.questionText: True or False: Even if you exceed the annual gift tax exclusion, you will likely owe gift tax immediately.
questions.explanation: Exceeding the annual exclusion does not automatically mean you owe gift tax. Instead, the overage reduces your lifetime federal exemption, which is $15 million per individual in 2026. Most people will never come close to that threshold. However, you are still required to file a gift tax return when you exceed the annual exclusion, even if no tax is owed. Failing to file is a separate issue from owing tax.

answers.answerText: True. Any gift over the annual limit is taxed at your marginal income tax rate.
False. Amounts over the annual limit reduce your lifetime exemption of $15 million before any tax is owed.
True. The IRS requires immediate payment on all gifts above the annual exclusion.
False. Gifts over the annual limit are simply not allowed under current tax law.

questions.questionText: True or False: Filing a tax extension means you have six more months to pay any taxes you owe.
questions.explanation: This is one of the most costly tax myths around. Filing Form 4868 gives you until October 15th to submit your return, but it does not extend your payment deadline. Any taxes you owe are still due by the original April deadline. If you underestimate and end up owing more when you file, you will owe interest and a late payment penalty on the difference. When in doubt, err on the side of overpaying your estimate to avoid penalties.

answers.answerText: False. An extension gives you more time to file your return, but any taxes owed are still due by the original April deadline.
True, but only if you are expecting a refund.
True. An extension gives you more time to both file and pay.
False. Extensions are only available to people who are waiting on missing tax documents.

questions.questionText: Does filing a tax extension increase your chances of being audited by the IRS?
questions.explanation: Filing an extension does not raise red flags with the IRS. In fact, it is commonly believed among tax professionals that filing an extension may slightly reduce your chances of a random audit, since certain audit selections can operate on a first-come, first-served basis. What can trigger an audit are the items on your return itself, not when you file it. Requesting an extension is a routine and fully accepted part of the tax system.

answers.answerText: Yes. Filing late is considered a red flag by the IRS.
Yes, but only if you have other potential red flags on your return.
No. Extensions are a normal part of the system and filing one does not trigger additional scrutiny.
No one knows. The IRS does not disclose how it selects returns for audit.

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about charitable contribution deductions is accurate for 2026?
questions.explanation: Starting in 2026, the rules around charitable deductions changed in important ways. Those who take the standard deduction can now deduct the first $1,000 in charitable contributions. Those who itemize can deduct charitable contributions above 0.5% of their Modified Adjusted Gross Income, though additional limits apply for those in the top 37% tax bracket. The gift must also be to a qualified tax-exempt organization, and if you received something of value in return, only the difference between what you gave and what you received is deductible.

answers.answerText: All gifts to any nonprofit organization are fully deductible regardless of what you receive in return.
Unlimited charitable deductions are available to everyone who makes a donation, whether they itemize or take the standard deduction.
You can only deduct a charitable contribution if you itemize, and your deduction is limited to what you gave minus the value of anything you received in return.

Starting in 2026, taxpayers who take the standard deduction can deduct up to $1,000 in charitable contributions, while those who itemize can deduct the full amount above 0.5% of their Modified Adjusted Gross Income.

criteria.label: More to Learn
criteria.resultText: Taxes are one of the areas where misconceptions cost people real money year after year. The good news is that most of what you can do to improve your tax situation does not require a law degree. Understanding the basics puts you in a much stronger position to make smarter decisions all year long, not just at tax time.
criteria.nextMove: Connect with a financial coach to review your tax strategy and make sure you are taking full advantage of the tax-advantaged accounts and planning opportunities available to you.

criteria.label: Tax Savvy
criteria.resultText: You have a strong grasp of how taxes actually work, and that knowledge is working in your favor. Most people never take the time to understand the rules, which means they leave money on the table every single year. The fact that you understand the difference between marginal and effective rates, gift tax rules, and filing deadlines puts you well ahead of the curve.

title: The Easiest Ways to Track Your Spending Without Losing Your Mind

biteSized.heading:
The Easiest Ways to Track Your Spending
Without Losing Your Mind
You're Not Alone
biteSized.body:
You want to be better at tracking spending, but it's just not happening. Don't feel bad. Many people have difficulty keeping track of where their money goes.

Here's the good news: there are several relatively easy ways to track where your money goes each month without losing your mind.

biteSized.heading: Use Your Debit Card or Credit Card
biteSized.body:
Those debit and credit card statements provide a handy history of exactly where your dollars went each month. Every time you swipe your card, your bank records where and how much you spent.

Tracking is as easy as logging into your online banking site or opening your monthly statement. Using your debit card avoids carrying lots of cash, debt and interest payments, and limits your spending to available cash.

biteSized.heading: Use a Cash-Back Rewards Credit Card
biteSized.body:
If you're already good about not spending more than you earn each month, you could use a cash-back rewards credit card to help track spending. It keeps detailed records of every transaction.

As a bonus, you might earn some cash back and give your credit score a healthy boost if you pay your balance in full each month. Look for a card with no annual fee that categorizes your transactions.

biteSized.heading: Write It Down
biteSized.body:
Old school may be old, but it still works. This method requires carrying pen and paper or remembering to jot notes on your smartphone.

Logging your spending helps you track how you spend cash, which card statements can't do. Psychologists confirm that writing things down is good for stress reduction and mental health. Use a spreadsheet to capture your monthly spending.

biteSized.heading: There's an App for That
biteSized.body: Several budgeting apps can link with your debit and credit cards, making expense tracking painless. Many are free or free to try. Check out some of the ones we think work best here.

biteSized.heading: What to Do Next
biteSized.body:
Before you can modify spending habits, you need to know where you typically spend money. Pick the method that feels most realistic for your life.

Start tracking for just one week to see where your money actually goes. You'll likely spot patterns that surprise you. Once you have that awareness, you can begin to control your spending.

title: Three Simple Systems That Actually Help You Control Your Spending

biteSized.heading:
Three Simple Systems
That Actually Help You Control Your Spending
Taking Control of Your Cash Flow
biteSized.body:
The most crucial step in managing money is figuring out how to control your cash flow. To turn that commitment into habits that stick, you need some strategies.

Try one or two of these systems to see what works best for you.

biteSized.heading: The Planned Spending Account System
biteSized.body:
If you feel like every time you start to get ahead, something random pops up, this method is for you.

Open a third savings account for planned expenses that don't occur monthly, like car maintenance, gifts, and vacations. Add up these costs for the year, divide by 12, and deposit that amount automatically each month.

Now when expenses come up, you have money set aside. No credit card needed.

biteSized.heading: The Cash Only System
biteSized.body:
Swiping a card makes it too easy to spend without feeling how much is going out.

Total your nonessential spending for the month, then divide by 4 to determine your weekly amount. Go to the ATM on the same day each week. When you go out, leave cards at home.

Handing over cash feels much more real than inserting your card. You'll rethink impulse buys.

biteSized.heading: The Envelope System
biteSized.body:
If you've been spending more on certain categories than you'd like, this system helps you stick to limits.

On payday, withdraw cash and divide it into labeled envelopes for each category like groceries, entertainment, and dining out. Once an envelope is empty, you're done spending in that category.

If you like this idea but not carrying cash, several apps use this concept.

biteSized.heading: Pick One and Commit
biteSized.body:
Choose one system and commit to trying it for one month. Start with the one that feels most doable. If one method isn't working, try another.

As long as you're feeling more in control of where your money is going, it's working.

title: The Investment Question That Could Cost You Thousands in Delayed Returns
content:
The clock starts ticking the moment someone realizes their money should be working for them. But there's a decision that needs to come first: Should they manage their own investment account or hand the reins to a financial advisor? Getting this answer right can determine how quickly wealth starts building. 

The Time Reality Check 

Honesty cuts through everything else here. Managing money demands real time. It's not just the minutes spent clicking “buy" or "sell," but the hours devoted to research, market monitoring, and maintenance. Matching financial goals to investment strategy requires homework that many people underestimate. The question isn't whether someone could do it. It's whether they actually will. 

The Knowledge Gap 

The second hurdle sits right behind the first: Does someone possess enough investment knowledge to construct a well-thought-out, manageable financial plan? If the answer isn't a confident “Yes” to both the time and knowledge issues, a financial professional may be best. 

When a Financial Advisor Makes Sense 

Time scarcity tops the list of reasons people seek advisors. Someone might have free hours available but still choose not to spend them tracking portfolios and analyzing market trends. That's a valid choice, not a weakness. 

Procrastination is another clear signal. Knowing exactly what to do means nothing if those tasks never get done. The best investment strategy in the world fails when it stays locked inside someone's head instead of being executed in their account. 

Discomfort with investment decisions rivals time constraints as a major factor. Self-doubt leads to paralysis, either making no moves at all or being too fearful to commit to investments. Both outcomes hurt long-term wealth building. 

Lack of discipline trips up even those with deep investment knowledge. Buying and selling investments requires a solid game plan tied to specific goals. Without one, people end up making moves at the wrong times. With a plan that gets abandoned, each deviation increases the chances of derailing progress toward financial goals. 

These situations aren't character flaws. They're practical realities that suggest a conversation with a financial advisor might be worth having. 

Finding the Right Advisor 

Once someone decides a financial advisor makes sense for their financial well-being, the homework shifts to finding someone reputable and trustworthy. Your employer may already offer access to trusted advisors, so start there. If not, ask for recommendations from financially responsible friends or family members as this often yields solid leads. 

For those without personal referrals, resources exist to help navigate the search. Guides on choosing a financial planner provide frameworks for evaluation. The National Association of Personal Financial Advisors at napfa.org offers a directory of fee-only financial planners. 

The investment decision isn't really about pride or independence. It's about recognizing what serves financial goals best. Sometimes that means going it alone. Sometimes it means finding the right person to help navigate the journey. Either way, making the choice quickly gets money working sooner rather than sitting idle while someone debates what to do.
excerpt: The Investment Question That Could Cost You Thousands in Delayed Returns

title: The 401(k) Summary Plan Description: Your Retirement Savings Roadmap
content:
A 401(k) Summary Plan Description (SPD) is a critical document that provides detailed information about a company’s 401(k) plan. It’s a comprehensive guide that explains the plan’s rules, benefits, and operations. The SPD (Summary Plan Description) is responsible for providing employees with enough information to enable them to make informed decisions about their retirement savings.

Understanding Your 401(k) Summary Plan Description

The SPD is a legally required document employers must provide participants and beneficiaries in their retirement plans. It outlines everything you need to know about your 401(k) plan, including its structure, the benefits it provides, and your rights and responsibilities as a plan participant.

The SPD covers several essential elements of your 401(k) plan.

  • It details the plan’s operation, including when you can join, how your contributions are calculated, and when you become vested.

  • It outlines the benefits of the plan, including the type of investments available and how to withdraw.

  • It outlines plan options such as 401(k) loans, rollover provisions, and distribution types.

  • It explains your rights and responsibilities, including your right to appeal a denied claim and your responsibility to inform your plan administrator about changes to your personal information.

Benefits of the 401(k) Summary Plan Description

The SPD serves multiple purposes that are beneficial to plan participants. It provides a clear, comprehensive outline of the plan’s workings, making it easier to understand the benefits and responsibilities associated with participation. It also serves as a valuable reference tool, allowing you to look up specific aspects of the plan as needed.

Having a thorough understanding of the information contained in the SPD can help you maximize your retirement savings. For example, knowing when you become fully vested can help you plan your employment and retirement strategy. Understanding the types of investments available can help you make informed decisions about allocating your funds.

Accessing Your 401(k) Summary Plan Description

Employers are required by law to provide you with a copy of the SPD within 90 days of becoming a participant in the plan. If there are changes to the plan, a revised SPD or a summary of material modifications (SMM) must be issued. You can also request a copy from your employer or plan administrator at any time.

Many employers also make these documents available online, either on the company’s intranet or on the website of the company that manages the 401(k) plan. In some cases, you might need to create an account to access these documents.

Your Roadmap to Retirement

The 401(k) Summary Plan Description is valuable for understanding and managing your retirement savings. It provides a detailed overview of your plan’s operation, benefits, and your rights and responsibilities. By familiarizing yourself with your SPD, you can make informed decisions that will help you maximize your 401(k) benefits and secure a comfortable retirement.
excerpt: With your SPD, you can make informed decisions that will help you maximize your 401(k) benefits and secure a comfortable retirement.

title: Saving on Taxes With A Homestead Exemption
content:
Being a homeowner comes with personal benefits, like the satisfaction of living in a space you own. For an added thrill, homeownership also comes with a few potential tax benefits! One way to take advantage of these tax benefits is through a Homestead Exemption.

A homestead exemption is a legal provision that gives homeowners a level of protection for their equity in their primary residence. This protection ensures that in times of financial distress, such as bankruptcy or foreclosure, a particular portion of the home’s value cannot be used to pay off outstanding debts or liabilities. This particular portion is excluded from the number used to calculate property taxes. While homestead exemptions can vary from state to state, with some having more requirements than others, they serve the common purpose of providing homeowners with some level of legal protection from creditors and other potential threats to their homeownership. 

For many homeowners, their primary residence is their largest financial investment. When facing financial hardships such as medical bills, credit card debt, or educational expenses, it can be challenging to maintain ownership of this investment. This is where a homestead exemption can come into play. While the level of protection offered by a homestead exemption varies, in general, homeowners can exempt a specific amount of the equity in their home from being used to pay off various debts. 

One of the primary benefits of a homestead exemption is that it can help individuals and families maintain ownership of their homes in times of financial crisis. Without a homestead exemption, creditors may be able to use the equity in the house to pay off outstanding debts or force the sale of the property. This could leave homeowners without a place to live and the financial resources needed to get back on track. With a homestead exemption, homeowners can safeguard their primary residence and maintain their ability to start fresh after tough financial times. 

In addition to protecting homeowners from creditors, a homestead exemption can also provide some financial benefits. Depending on the state, a homestead exemption may help homeowners reduce their property tax burden. This is because the home’s value is lower once the exemption is calculated, which can result in a lower tax bill. While this may not be a significant cost savings for all homeowners, it is a nice bonus for those struggling to make ends meet. 

To take advantage of a homestead exemption, homeowners typically need to file the appropriate paperwork with their county or state government. Eligibility requirements vary, but homeowners must own and occupy their primary residence. 

While the rules and regulations surrounding homestead exemptions can be complex, the benefits are clear. A homestead exemption can help homeowners and families maintain ownership of their homes during tough financial times by providing them with a certain level of legal protection and financial benefits. This can be particularly important for those in areas with high housing costs or rapidly rising property values. In these situations, a homestead exemption can help homeowners keep up with homeownership expenses and build long-term wealth. 

If you are interested in learning more about how a homestead exemption could benefit you, it may be helpful to speak with a real estate lawyer or financial advisor or check out this article on applying for an exemption. They can provide more detailed guidance on the specific eligibility requirements and benefits of a homestead exemption in your state. With the right information and guidance, homeowners can take advantage of this legal provision to protect their most important asset and maintain their financial stability even during difficult times.
excerpt: Homestead exemptions provide homeowners with some level of legal protection from creditors and other potential threats to their homeownership as well as a reduced property tax bill.

title: Getting Ready To Retire: Withdrawal Strategies Action Plan
excerpt: This action plan will help you navigate making savvy withdrawals as you get ready to retire.
content:
Step 1: Setting the stage

Take Action: 

  • Know the key ages. There are specific ages in which you can make strategic withdrawals to save on taxes and maximize Social Security benefits. This will help you match these ages with your expected longevity.

  • Gather information. Collect numbers from expected income sources, such as social security and pensions. Get your social security statement from ssa.gov. It provides a personalized monthly retirement estimate and a record of earnings from eligible income. Include additional benefits such as Disability and Survivor benefits. 

  • Envision your retirement lifestyle. Create a retirement budget that includes your income and expenses. Prepare for changing needs like health, income/lifestyle, and relationship changes.

  • Determine if you have an income gap. Using the numbers you collected from your income sources, subtract your expenses and determine if there is a gap in which income does not cover all expenses. 

Step 2: Strategies for withdrawing

Take Action:

  • Withdraw from a 401(k). Explore whether a partial or total distribution makes sense for you and your budget or if deferring your payments allows you to stretch your income further. 

  • Evaluate different withdrawal strategies.  Consider the 4% rule where withdrawals are set at 4% the first year and then adjusted for inflation going forward.  Other strategies to consider are withdrawing a fixed dollar amount, fixed percentage, interest/dividends only, or just the RMD amount each year. 

  • Supplement with annuities. An annuity provides you with a guaranteed income stream suitable for conservative investors, those with long life expectancy, and those needing a fixed income stream. Be aware of the fine print, including fees, and ensure your annuity is part of a well-diversified investment portfolio.

  • Explore the bucket strategy. This is also called the time-based segmentation approach. With this strategy, you think of your money in 3 hypothetical buckets that are aligned with your spending needs and the timeline for those needs.

Step 3: Taxation of your income and withdrawals

Take Action:

  • Understand how income will be taxed.

    • Social Security is taxed based on factors such as marital status, income, and your state. The maximum taxable amount is 85%

    • Consider lifestyle changes that impact your taxes, such as relocating, cost of living, healthcare costs, housing expenses, state taxes, and travel.  

  • Maximize tax efficiency on withdrawals today. If your goal is to maximize tax efficiency during your lifetime, explore ways to reduce your tax bill, such as reimbursing medical expenses in your HSA, using Roth withdrawals, non-retirement accounts and savings withdrawals.  

  • Maximize tax efficiency for your heirs. If you are focused instead on maximizing tax efficiency for your heirs, consider withdrawals from pre-tax accounts first, Roth conversions, and preserving assets in non-retirement accounts for a step up in basis.

If you plan to retire in the next 10 years, here are some of the most important action steps you can take now to prepare for retirement:

Steps to take now to prepare for retirement

1. Write down your life goals, not just your retirement goals

Set goals across all areas of your life including family, career/volunteer, social, health, spiritual/personal growth, and learning. I’ve always believed it’s not a meaningful goal unless you put it in writing.

2. Review your personal spending plan

Examine your current spending using online budgeting tools such as PocketGuard. The old-school paper and pencil method also works, as does the Easy Spending Plan calculator. Once you’ve identified where your money is going, you can try to free up some extra money to save and invest for retirement.

You can also go ahead and create a Budget Plan for Retirement to identify areas of spending like health care or travel that may be changing the most during your financial freedom years.

3. Maximize your retirement savings to tax-advantaged accounts

The IRS adjusts the contribution limits each year. 

You can also consider contributing to a traditional or Roth IRA (lower limits).

 

4. Run multiple retirement calculations

Estimating your retirement outlook is a best-practice financial planning activity and should be done at least once a year. Be sure to use multiple calculators to account for different assumptions and methodologies. You can often find a good calculator built into your 401(s) plan or you can use this retirement plan estimator.

5. Review your health insurance coverage options

This is the number one concern for soon-to-be retirees for a reason. Health-related costs are a significant portion of the budget during retirement. If you have retiree medical insurance, go ahead and start reviewing your options and the associated costs.

You should also visit the healthcare.gov site if you will be retiring prior to age 65 when Medicare eligibility kicks in. If you are in a high deductible plan with an HSA option, take full advantage of your ability to set aside the maximum amount of pre-tax dollars to help cover future costs.

If you are, or will be 65 when you retire, then you need to learn the basics of Medicare and which options may make sense for you. 

6. Obtain long-term care insurance

Long-term care costs can be a significant drain on a family’s net worth. You can choose to either self-insure with your retirement nest egg, spend down assets to qualify for Medicaid, or purchase long-term care insurance. To learn more about long-term care insurance, check out lifehappens.org or longtermcare.gov.

7. Have a plan to eliminate your debt (including the mortgage)

What do many financially confident retirees have in common? They are debt-free!  You can use the Debt Blaster Calculator to help you coordinate the timing of your mortgage elimination plan.

8. Re-Assess your tolerance for risk

It’s natural to invest more conservatively as we age, but the pendulum can shift too much in the flight to safety, and longer life expectancies increase the risk of outliving our money. An extreme move to conservative investment options may not always be prudent in a rising interest rate environment. Many financial planners consider Social Security as part of the fixed income portion of your total retirement portfolio so the remaining assets can be more aggressive.

Check where you are at by taking a Risk Tolerance Assessment.

9. Update your estate planning documents

What worked during your 30’s may not be the best plan in your 50’s and 60’s. That is why it is important to make sure you have crucial estate planning documents in place, such as a will, durable power of attorney, health care power of attorney, and advanced health care directive. Check out these 5 Steps to Estate Planning to make sure you have your own estate plan (rather than relying on the intestacy laws of the state you live in).

10. Estimate your pension (if you have one) and Social Security income

If you are eligible for a pension through your current or former employer, you should go ahead and estimate your benefit. Start weighing the pros and cons of choosing a monthly pension vs a lump sum option. Next, estimate your Social Security so you know the total sum of your guaranteed income sources. (Yeah, I know that I used the word guarantee in the same sentence as Social Security, but long-term funding issues are less of a concern if you are retiring within the next 10 years.)

The action steps listed above are best practice retirement planning behaviors regardless of your age or proximity to retirement. However, the sense of urgency definitely goes up once that retirement date is 10 years or less away. Don’t wait until the last minute to get started with your own retirement planning. Even if you don’t feel too confident about your ability to retire on your terms, a little proactive planning can help you see how to create a flexible plan that fits your life goals.
excerpt: If you plan to retire in the next 10 years, here are some of the most important action steps you can take now to prepare for retirement:
title: Retiring Within the Next 10 Years? 10 Things to Do Now

content:
Deferred compensation plans have become an integral way to save for retirement. They come in two general forms. The first is a qualified deferred compensation plan governed by ERISA rules, including 401(k) and 403(b) plans. The second form is the less common non-qualified deferred compensation plan (NQDC), also known as IRS section 409A plans. Although NQDC plans are less familiar relative to qualified plans, they have become increasingly common as an executive perk due to their potential tax benefits and their ability to help companies retain key employees.  

Let’s break down the non-qualified deferred compensation plan to help you better understand what they are, how they work, and if participating makes sense for you.     

What is a Non-Qualified Deferred Compensation Plan (NQDC), and how does it work?  

An NQDC plan allows an employee to defer earned compensation to a later date. It is compensation earned by the employee that they have not received yet from their employer. In most cases, taxes on this income are deferred until the employer pays out the compensation since the compensation is still technically the company’s property.  

A person may opt for deferred compensation because it offers potential tax benefits. In a nutshell, those tax benefits are realized when the payment and the taxes are deferred. Ideally, this is when the employee expects to be in a lower tax bracket, typically due to retirement or a reduction in income, which gives them the best chance to reduce their total tax bill significantly.  

Key Points to be aware of:   

  • The employee must make an irrevocable election to defer compensation before the year in which they earn the salary.   
  • The amount paid, payment schedule, and the triggering event resulting in payment are specified at the time of deferral.  
  • The six permissible triggering events are a fixed date, separation from service (i.e., retirement), a change in ownership or control of the company, disability, death, or an unforeseen emergency.  
  • The NQDC plan can also impose other conditions such as non-compete clauses and refraining from providing advisory services after retirement.  
  • The compensation in the plan is technically seen as an unsecured loan between the lending employee and the borrowing employer. An employer promises to pay in the future. As a result, there is a substantial risk of forfeiture if the company were to get in any financial trouble.  

Should I participate?  

Determining whether you should participate hinges upon how you answer a few key questions.  

  1. What is the financial strength of your employer? NQDC plans are an IOU from your employer. If the company goes bankrupt, NQDC is considered an unsecured debt, which may mean a total loss of your contribution.  
  2. How much of your wealth have you tied to your employer? This question begs you to consider your income and wealth concentration risk. In addition to salary, you may have stock options, restricted stock units, or stock purchase plans, all of which are tied into the company’s future. Adding NQDC further increases your exposure and risk here.   
  3. How long before you plan to retire or leave your current employer? The longer you have until a triggering event like retirement, the greater your employer’s risk of financial instability. During times of economic crisis, which are very difficult to predict (The Covid-19 Pandemic), the financial health of even the most prominent companies can quickly deteriorate. Remember Chrysler in 2008 and Hertz in 2020?  
  4. Consider what your tax bracket is now versus what it might be in the future. Can deferring now put you in a lower tax bracket? And considering all future sources of income, what is your tax bracket likely to be in retirement? This is particularly hard since no one knows for sure what tax rates or brackets will be in five, 10, or 15 plus years. Speaking to a qualified tax professional can be critical in guiding you on this type of planning.  

When should I take it?  

The primary motivation for participating in a deferred compensation plan is to lower your taxes. Therefore, your timing should coincide with when you think you will derive the greatest tax benefit, i.e., when your income would be lowest or at least lower (according to marginal tax brackets).  current-year-income-tax-brackets snippet 

Take time to think through your future expense needs and your plans to continue working, in addition to your social security withdrawal strategy.  

NQDC can be a great way to cover the gap between retirement and claiming social security so that you can benefit from the guaranteed income increases (8% after Full retirement age) while paying less in taxes.   

How should I take it?  

How to take your payments (i.e., lump-sum distributions or equal distributions over a period of years) should be dictated by your expense needs, your other forms of income, and the resultant tax bill they could generate.  

Take time to estimate your retirement expense needs, and be sure to consider health care expenses and any other obligations that might come up during retirement. You can use a worksheet like this to help you get organized, list out all income resources and options, and consider the potential tax implications. Again, a qualified tax professional can be crucial here.  

In Summary  

NQDC plans are a great way to save more for retirement and save on taxes. However, to have the greatest chance of success, be sure to invest your time to think through the pros and cons and how this opportunity fits into your financial plan.  

If you need help deciding, don’t hesitate to consult with a qualified tax professional or a trusted financial planning professional to guide you in the process.
excerpt: Deferred compensation plans have become an integral way to save for retirement. They come in two general forms. The
title: Does a Deferred Compensation Plan Make Sense For You?
